wine_history.name: Phoenician traders found Gadir, c. 1100 BC
wine_history.description: Phoenician merchants from Tyre established Gadir (modern Cadiz) around 1100 BC, making it the oldest continuously inhabited city in western Europe. They brought vine cultivation techniques from the eastern Mediterranean and traded wine across the Atlantic coast. The albariza chalk soils of the Cadiz hinterland proved well-suited to white grape cultivation.
wine_history.evidence: The Sherry Wine Council and Consejo Regulador de Jerez-Xeres-Sherry document the Phoenician foundation of Gadir as the earliest documented viticultural presence in the region.

wine_history.name: Roman Hispania Baetica and Columella, 1st century AD
wine_history.description: Under Roman rule the province of Hispania Baetica, centred on modern Andalucia, became one of the empire's principal wine exporting zones. The agronomist Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella was born at Gades (Cadiz) around 4 AD and wrote De Re Rustica, the most influential agricultural treatise of antiquity, which devotes three books to viticulture. Amphorae stamped with Baetican origins have been found at Hadrian's Wall and Rhine frontier forts.
wine_history.evidence: Columella's Cadiz birth is recorded in his own texts and confirmed by the Oxford Classical Dictionary. The Wine Council of Jerez cites the Roman viticulture legacy on its history page.

wine_history.name: Moorish Al-Andalus and the origins of distillation, 711-1264
wine_history.description: Arab rulers of Al-Andalus gave modern viticulture two lasting legacies: the word alcohol, derived from al-kuhul (a fine antimony powder, later applied to the essence of distilled spirit), and the alembic still, which underpins sherry's fortification practice. Despite Islamic restrictions on consumption, grape cultivation for raisins and vinegar continued, and historical evidence suggests flor yeast cultivation predates the Christian reconquest. The Moorish name for Jerez, Sherish, became the English Sherry.
wine_history.evidence: The etymological derivation of alcohol from al-kuhul and alembic from al-anbiq is documented in the Oxford English Dictionary and confirmed by the Sherry Wine Council's historical narrative.

wine_history.name: Christian reconquest of Jerez by Alfonso X, 1264
wine_history.description: Alfonso X of Castile reconquered Jerez de la Frontera from Moorish rule in 1264. The Christian crown redistributed vineyards to monastic orders and noble families, and wine trade with northern Europe expanded through the port of Sanlucar de Barrameda. The town's position on the frontier between Christian Castile and the Moorish kingdom of Granada gave it the Frontera epithet that remains in its name today.
wine_history.evidence: The reconquest date and Alfonso X's role are confirmed by the municipality of Jerez and the Consejo Regulador historical record.

wine_history.name: The British sack market and Shakespearean fame, 16th century
wine_history.description: English merchants called the oxidatively-aged white wine from Jerez sack, probably from the Spanish sacar (to export). By the 1560s sack was the most fashionable drink in England, immortalised by Shakespeare in Henry IV Part II where Falstaff delivers his eulogy: a good sherris-sack hath a two-fold operation in it... it ascendeth me into the brain. The Bristol merchants John Stubbs and John Blake are among the earliest documented English shippers in the Jerez trade.
wine_history.evidence: The Shakespeare quote from Henry IV Part II Act 4 Scene 3 is the earliest printed English reference to sherris-sack. The Consejo Regulador and multiple wine historians cite it as the origin of the English word sherry.

wine_history.name: Francis Drake raids Cadiz and seeds the British sherry market, 1587
wine_history.description: In April and May 1587, Sir Francis Drake led an English naval expedition into Cadiz harbour, destroying 37 Spanish vessels in what he called singeing the King of Spain's beard. Among the spoils his fleet loaded were approximately 2,900 butts of sherry destined for the Armada's provisioning. Returning to England, Drake sold the cargo and effectively created the British sherry import market at scale. The episode is the single most consequential commercial event in sherry's history, cementing the British role in the trade for the next three centuries.
wine_history.evidence: Drake's raid and the 2,900 butts figure are documented by multiple historians including Hugh Johnson in The Story of Wine and are referenced by the Consejo Regulador's historical narrative.

wine_history.name: British and Irish shipping houses established, 17th-18th century
wine_history.description: Following Drake's raid, British and Irish merchant families settled permanently in Jerez, El Puerto de Santa Maria and Sanlucar de Barrameda to manage the sherry trade directly. Major houses founded in this era include Sandeman (1790), Williams and Humbert (1877), and Harvey (1796 in Bristol, with Bristol Cream developed in the 19th century). These foreign shipping dynasties introduced the solera ageing system and bodega architecture to the triangle and shaped sherry's global identity as a British cultural object.
wine_history.evidence: Individual founding dates are confirmed by the houses' own histories. The Consejo Regulador history page describes the British merchant colony and its role in developing the solera system.

wine_history.name: Osborne founded at El Puerto de Santa Maria, 1772
wine_history.description: Thomas Osborne Mann, an Englishman from Exeter, founded the Osborne bodega at El Puerto de Santa Maria in 1772, making it one of the oldest continuously operating wine companies in Spain. The house pioneered the fino and amontillado styles in El Puerto and later became famous across Spain for its black bull roadside hoardings designed by Manolo Prieto in 1956, which became a national icon after appearing along Spanish highways for sherry advertising.
wine_history.evidence: The 1772 founding date is confirmed on Osborne's own history page. The Consejo Regulador lists Osborne among its founding-era member bodegas.

wine_history.name: Gonzalez Byass and the founding of Tio Pepe, 1835
wine_history.description: Manuel Maria Gonzalez Angel founded Bodegas Gonzalez Byass in Jerez in 1835 and named the house's flagship fino after his uncle Jose Angel Gonzalez, known as Tio Pepe (Uncle Joe). Tio Pepe became the world's best-selling fino sherry and transformed the style into a global brand. Gonzalez Byass remains family-owned today and the bodega complex adjacent to the Jerez Alcazar is an architectural landmark of the Sherry Triangle.
wine_history.evidence: The 1835 founding and the Tio Pepe naming story are confirmed on the Gonzalez Byass official website and the Consejo Regulador member register.

wine_history.name: DO Jerez established: Spain's first Denominacion de Origen, 1933
wine_history.description: The Consejo Regulador de Jerez-Xeres-Sherry was formally established in 1933, making Jerez the first Denominacion de Origen in Spain and an early statutory wine appellation in Europe. The DO defined the Sherry Triangle (Jerez, El Puerto de Santa Maria and Sanlucar de Barrameda) as the production zone, mandated the solera system, and established minimum ageing requirements for fino, oloroso, amontillado and palo cortado styles.
wine_history.evidence: The 1933 founding date and the first-DO-in-Spain status are confirmed on the Consejo Regulador's official website and in multiple official historical accounts.

wine_history.name: The solera system and biological ageing under flor codified, 19th century
wine_history.description: The solera system of fractional blending, in which older and younger wines are progressively blended through a stack of barrels, was formalised in Jerez during the 19th century. Equally distinctive is biological ageing under flor yeast (Saccharomyces beticus) that forms on the wine's surface, protecting it from oxidation and imparting a distinctive saline, almond and fresh-dough character. The flor yeast is native to the Sherry Triangle and maintained at industrial scale only in this region.
wine_history.evidence: The solera system and flor biology are described in detail by the Consejo Regulador and by the Oxford Companion to Wine (Jancis Robinson) as fundamental to Jerez's wine identity.

wine_history.name: Manzanilla de Sanlucar: a distinct DO within the Triangle, 1964
wine_history.description: Manzanilla, the biologically aged fino-style sherry produced exclusively in Sanlucar de Barrameda at the mouth of the Guadalquivir river, received its own Denominacion de Origen Manzanilla-Sanlucar de Barrameda in 1964. This formally recognized that the Atlantic humidity, lower temperatures and sea breezes in Sanlucar's bodegas produce a lighter, saltier, more delicate expression of the flor-aged style than inland fino produced in Jerez or El Puerto.
wine_history.evidence: The Manzanilla DO distinction and 1964 origin are confirmed by the Consejo Regulador and documented on the Sherry Wine Council website.

wine_history.name: The sherry crisis and the Jerez Plan, 1970s-1980s
wine_history.description: After decades of volume-led bulk exports primarily to the United Kingdom, the sherry industry entered a severe crisis in the late 1970s caused by falling demand, oversupply from vineyard expansion to 22,000 hectares, and cheap bulk bottling that damaged the category's image. A structural restructuring plan, the Jerez Plan, reduced the DO's vineyard area from approximately 22,000 hectares to around 7,000 hectares by the late 1980s and shifted emphasis toward quality over volume. Several major British shipping houses exited the market or merged.
wine_history.evidence: The vineyard contraction from 22,000 to approximately 7,000 hectares is documented by the Consejo Regulador and multiple trade historians. The Sherry Wine Council's own history page describes the restructuring.

wine_history.name: VOS and VORS certification with carbon dating, 1990s
wine_history.description: The Consejo Regulador introduced the VOS (Vinum Optimum Signatum, 20 years average age) and VORS (Vinum Optimum Rare Signatum, 30 years average age) categories. Because sherry ages fractionally under the solera system rather than as discrete vintages, age verification uses carbon-14 radioactive dating to confirm the average age of the wine blend. This made sherry one of the few wine categories where scientific carbon dating is mandatory quality certification.
wine_history.evidence: The VOS and VORS categories with carbon-dating methodology are confirmed by the Consejo Regulador Jerez and described on the Sherry Wine Council website.

wine_history.name: Equipo Navazos and the modern sherry revival, 2005
wine_history.description: Sherry scholar Jesus Barquin and winemaker Eduardo Ojeda founded Equipo Navazos in 2005 as a negociant project dedicated to bottling exceptional single-cask sherries from bonded warehouses across the Triangle under the La Bota series. The project focused critical and wine-enthusiast attention back onto extraordinary old-solera stocks sitting undervalued in the great bodegas, and is widely credited as the catalyst for the modern sherry revival.
wine_history.evidence: The 2005 founding of Equipo Navazos and Jesus Barquin and Eduardo Ojeda's roles are confirmed on the Equipo Navazos official website and widely covered in wine media.

wine_history.name: The en rama renaissance: unfiltered sherry, mid-2000s
wine_history.description: The en rama style, in which fino and manzanilla sherry is bottled with minimal fining and filtration directly from the solera, was championed in the mid-2000s principally by Lustau, whose first commercial en rama bottling appeared in 2000, and by Hidalgo La Gitana whose Manzanilla Pasada en Rama gained a wide following. En rama sherries show a richer, more complex profile compared to the standard filtered release, and seasonal bottling windows became events in the wine calendar.
wine_history.evidence: Lustau's en rama releases and Hidalgo La Gitana's role are documented by multiple wine journalists and confirmed on the producers' own websites. The Sherry Wine Council describes en rama as a distinct style.

wine_history.name: The single-vineyard pago movement and albariza rediscovery, 2010s
wine_history.description: A new generation of Jerez producers led by Ramiro Ibanez (Bodegas Cota 45), Willy Perez (De la Riva), and Luis Perez began bottling single-pago wines from named albariza vineyard plots, a concept previously unknown in the sherry tradition. The movement drew on Champagne and Burgundy terroir models to celebrate the distinctions between Jerez's chalk-soil parcels: Balbainas, Carrascal, Macharnudo, Anina, Balbaina Alta. These pago wines transformed international perception of sherry's terroir potential.
wine_history.evidence: The single-pago pioneers and named albariza plots are documented by Tim Atkin MW, Jancis Robinson MW, and the producers' own websites. Bodegas Cota 45 publishes vineyard-specific information.

wine_history.name: Pedro Ximenez and the sweet sherry tradition
wine_history.description: Pedro Ximenez grapes are sun-dried on esparto grass mats in the asoleo process for 10-14 days after harvest to concentrate sugars to extraordinary levels before fermentation. The resulting wine, with natural sugar levels above 400 grams per litre and a jet-black colour, is among the most concentrated dessert-wine styles produced commercially. The Montilla-Moriles region supplies the majority of PX grapes to Jerez under a long-standing cross-DO agreement.
wine_history.evidence: The asoleo process and Montilla-Moriles cross-DO supply are confirmed by the Consejo Regulador and by the Sherry Wine Council's style descriptions.

wine_history.name: Aponiente places Jerez on the global gastronomy map, 2015
wine_history.description: Chef Angel Leon relocated Aponiente to a restored tidal mill in El Puerto de Santa Maria in 2015 and built the restaurant's wine programme entirely around sherry pairings. Holding three Michelin stars since 2017, Aponiente demonstrated that fino, manzanilla, oloroso and palo cortado can serve as serious food wines at the highest culinary level, attracting wine-and-food travellers to the Sherry Triangle from around the world.
wine_history.evidence: Aponiente's three-star status and sherry-pairing programme are confirmed by the Michelin Guide. The El Puerto de Santa Maria location and Angel Leon's role are confirmed on the restaurant's own website.

wine_history.name: Albariza chalk soils: global terroir recognition, present
wine_history.description: The white albariza chalk soils unique to the Jerez Superior zone are now recognized by the Consejo Regulador and a body of viticultural research as a distinctive Andalusian terroir. Composed of up to 80 percent calcium carbonate laid down in the Miocene epoch, albariza acts as a natural sponge that retains winter rainfall and releases it slowly through the dry summer to the Palomino Fino vine's roots. The Consejo Regulador has pursued UNESCO heritage status for the albariza landscape and sherry production system.
wine_history.evidence: Albariza soil composition and the UNESCO application are confirmed by the Consejo Regulador and by the Sherry Wine Council's terroir pages.
